quiz_questions.question_text: What is a reference frame?

options.text: A frame that is always at rest
The boundary of an experimental setup
A coordinate system used to describe the position and motion of objects
A type of picture frame used in physics labs

quiz_questions.explanation: A reference frame is a coordinate system (with an origin and axes) relative to which we measure positions, velocities, and accelerations of objects.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you are sitting on a moving train, what is your velocity relative to the train?

options.text: Zero — you are at rest relative to the train
Equal to the train's speed
Depends on the direction you face
Equal to the speed of sound

quiz_questions.explanation: In the reference frame of the train, you are not moving — your velocity relative to the train is zero. You are at rest in that frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car moves east at 60 km/h and a truck moves east at 40 km/h. What is the velocity of the car relative to the truck?

options.text: 60 km/h east
40 km/h west
100 km/h east
20 km/h east

quiz_questions.explanation: v_car relative to truck = v_car − v_truck = 60 − 40 = 20 km/h east. From the truck's perspective, the car moves ahead at 20 km/h.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two cars approach each other, each moving at 50 km/h. What is their relative velocity?

options.text: 0 km/h
50 km/h
25 km/h
100 km/h — they approach each other at the sum of their speeds

quiz_questions.explanation: When objects move toward each other, relative speed = sum of speeds. If car A moves east at 50 and car B west at 50, v_A relative to B = 50 − (−50) = 100 km/h.

quiz_questions.question_text: A person walks at 1 m/s on a conveyor belt moving at 2 m/s in the same direction. What is their speed relative to the ground?

options.text: 3 m/s
1 m/s
2 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: When moving in the same direction, velocities add: v_person/ground = v_person/belt + v_belt/ground = 1 + 2 = 3 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an inertial reference frame?

options.text: A frame where all objects are at rest
A frame that rotates with the Earth
Any frame attached to the Earth
A reference frame that is not accelerating — it moves at constant velocity or is at rest

quiz_questions.explanation: An inertial reference frame is one in which Newton's first law holds — objects at rest stay at rest and moving objects maintain constant velocity unless acted upon by a force. It is non-accelerating.

quiz_questions.question_text: A boat crosses a river at 5 m/s relative to the water. The river flows at 3 m/s. What is the boat's speed relative to the ground?

options.text: 2 m/s
√(5² + 3²) = √34 ≈ 5.83 m/s — the velocities add as perpendicular vectors
8 m/s
5 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the boat moves perpendicular to the river flow, the velocities add as vectors: v = √(5² + 3²) = √34 ≈ 5.83 m/s.

quiz_questions.question_text: If you throw a ball forward at 10 m/s while riding in a car moving at 20 m/s, what is the ball's speed relative to the ground?

options.text: 15 m/s
10 m/s
20 m/s
30 m/s — velocities add when in the same direction

quiz_questions.explanation: v_ball/ground = v_ball/car + v_car/ground = 10 + 20 = 30 m/s (when thrown in the direction of motion).

quiz_questions.question_text: Is the Earth an inertial reference frame?

options.text: Yes — it is perfectly inertial
Approximately yes for most everyday situations, though technically it rotates and orbits
No — it can never be treated as inertial
Only at the equator

quiz_questions.explanation: The Earth rotates and orbits the Sun, making it technically non-inertial. However, for most everyday physics problems, the effects of Earth's rotation are negligible, so it is treated as approximately inertial.

quiz_questions.question_text: A passenger in a car throws a ball straight up. From the passenger's perspective, the ball goes straight up and down. What does a person standing on the roadside see?

options.text: The ball moves backward
The ball goes straight up and down
The ball follows a parabolic path — it has horizontal velocity from the car and vertical velocity from the throw
The ball moves only horizontally

quiz_questions.explanation: In the car's frame, the ball goes straight up and down. From the ground frame, the ball has both the car's horizontal velocity and the vertical throw velocity, creating a parabolic trajectory.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a reference frame?

options.text: The boundary of an experiment
A unit of measurement
A wooden frame holding lab equipment
A coordinate system and associated time standard attached to an observer, used to measure positions, velocities, and other physical quantities

quiz_questions.explanation: A reference frame provides the axes and origin from which an observer measures physical quantities. Different observers in different frames may measure different values for the same event.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes relative velocity?

options.text: The velocity of an object relative to the ground only
The maximum speed of an object
The average of two objects' velocities
The velocity of one object as measured by or with respect to another object's reference frame, calculated by vector subtraction of their velocities

quiz_questions.explanation: Relative velocity v_A/B = v_A − v_B gives how fast A appears to move from B's perspective. It is a vector quantity that depends on the chosen reference frame.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an inertial reference frame?

options.text: Any frame at rest
A reference frame in which Newton's laws of motion hold in their simplest form — one that is not accelerating or rotating
A frame centered at the Sun
A frame where objects always accelerate

quiz_questions.explanation: In an inertial frame, an object with no net force has zero acceleration (Newton's first law). Any frame moving at constant velocity relative to an inertial frame is also inertial.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a non-inertial reference frame?

options.text: A frame used only in space
A frame that is always at rest
A reference frame that is accelerating or rotating, in which fictitious forces must be introduced for Newton's laws to appear valid
A frame where no objects are moving

quiz_questions.explanation: In a non-inertial (accelerating) frame, objects appear to experience forces that have no physical source — these are called fictitious or pseudo-forces (e.g., centrifugal force, Coriolis force).

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes Galilean transformation?

options.text: A method of rotating coordinate axes
A transformation used only by Galileo in the 17th century
The conversion from Celsius to Fahrenheit
The set of equations that relate position, velocity, and time measurements between two inertial reference frames moving at constant velocity relative to each other

quiz_questions.explanation: Galilean transformations: x' = x − vt, t' = t. They assume absolute time and are accurate at low speeds. At speeds near light, they are replaced by Lorentz transformations.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes a fictitious force?

options.text: An apparent force that appears in a non-inertial reference frame to account for the frame's acceleration — it has no physical agent or source
A force measured incorrectly by a scientist
A force that is imaginary and has no effects
Any force that is very small

quiz_questions.explanation: Fictitious (pseudo) forces like centrifugal force, Coriolis force, and the force you feel when a car brakes are real in their effects within the non-inertial frame but have no physical cause — they arise from the frame's acceleration.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the Galilean velocity addition formula?

options.text: A formula only valid for rotating frames
The equation v_AC = v_AB + v_BC, which states that the velocity of A relative to C equals the velocity of A relative to B plus the velocity of B relative to C
The equation for gravitational velocity
An equation for adding positions

quiz_questions.explanation: Galilean velocity addition is a chain rule for velocities: v_AC = v_AB + v_BC. This works well at everyday speeds but breaks down near the speed of light.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes an observer in physics?

options.text: A camera pointed at a moving object
Only a human watching an experiment
A scientist who does not participate in experiments
A person or measuring device in a specific reference frame that records positions, times, and other physical quantities of events

quiz_questions.explanation: In physics, an 'observer' is anyone (or any device) that measures physical quantities from a given reference frame. Different observers in different frames may record different measurements.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes the principle of Galilean relativity?

options.text: The principle that the laws of mechanics are the same in all inertial reference frames — no mechanical experiment can distinguish between uniform motion and rest
A principle applicable only to light
The idea that only Earth's frame is valid
The principle that everything is relative

quiz_questions.explanation: Galilean relativity states that no experiment within a closed, uniformly moving room can determine whether the room is at rest or in uniform motion. The laws of mechanics are identical in all inertial frames.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which definition BEST describes velocity composition?

options.text: The process of combining velocity vectors from different reference frames to find an object's velocity in a third frame
Adding the speeds of two objects together
Decomposing velocity into components
Finding the average velocity of a group

quiz_questions.explanation: Velocity composition (addition) allows us to find velocities across reference frames. For three frames A, B, C: v_AC = v_AB + v_BC. At relativistic speeds, the formula changes.

quiz_questions.question_text: A plane flies north at 200 km/h relative to the air. A wind blows east at 50 km/h. What is the plane's speed relative to the ground?

options.text: √(200² + 50²) = √42500 ≈ 206.2 km/h — the perpendicular velocities combine by the Pythagorean theorem
200 km/h
250 km/h
150 km/h

quiz_questions.explanation: Ground velocity = vector sum of airplane velocity and wind velocity. Since they are perpendicular: v = √(200² + 50²) = √42500 ≈ 206.2 km/h.

quiz_questions.question_text: A swimmer swims at 2 m/s in still water. She tries to swim straight across a river flowing at 1.5 m/s. At what angle downstream from her intended path does she actually travel?

options.text: arctan(2/1.5) ≈ 53.1° downstream
0° — she goes straight across
90° — she goes entirely downstream
arctan(1.5/2) ≈ 36.9° downstream — the river pushes her sideways

quiz_questions.explanation: tan(θ) = v_river/v_swimmer = 1.5/2 = 0.75, so θ = arctan(0.75) ≈ 36.9° downstream from her intended perpendicular path.

quiz_questions.question_text: Train A moves east at 80 km/h and Train B moves west at 60 km/h on parallel tracks. What is the velocity of Train A as observed from Train B?

options.text: 140 km/h west
20 km/h east
140 km/h east — v_A/B = v_A − v_B = 80 − (−60) = 140 km/h east
80 km/h east

quiz_questions.explanation: Taking east as positive: v_A = +80, v_B = −60. v_A/B = v_A − v_B = 80 − (−60) = 140 km/h east.

quiz_questions.question_text: You run at 3 m/s on a moving walkway that moves at 2 m/s in the same direction. Then you turn around and run back at 3 m/s relative to the walkway. What are your ground speeds in each case?

options.text: 5 m/s forward and 1 m/s forward — going with: 3 + 2 = 5; going against: 2 − 3 = −1 m/s (actually 3 − 2 = 1 m/s backward relative to your original direction... but the walkway still carries you at 2 m/s forward, so net is 2 − 3 = −1 m/s, meaning 1 m/s backward)
Going with: 5 m/s forward; Running back: 1 m/s backward relative to the ground (net: −3 + 2 = −1 m/s, i.e., 1 m/s opposite to walkway direction)
3 m/s and 3 m/s
5 m/s and 5 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: Forward: v = 3 + 2 = 5 m/s. Backward (running against walkway): v = −3 + 2 = −1 m/s, meaning 1 m/s in the backward direction relative to the ground. The walkway partially cancels your backward running.

quiz_questions.question_text: Two boats leave from the same point. Boat A goes north at 4 m/s and Boat B goes east at 3 m/s. What is the velocity of Boat A relative to Boat B?

options.text: 7 m/s north
5 m/s northeast
1 m/s northeast
5 m/s in a direction 53.1° west of north — v_A/B = v_A − v_B, giving a vector pointing north and west with magnitude √(4² + 3²) = 5 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: v_A/B = v_A − v_B = (0, 4) − (3, 0) = (−3, 4). Magnitude = √(9 + 16) = 5 m/s. Direction: arctan(3/4) ≈ 36.9° west of north.

quiz_questions.question_text: Raindrops fall vertically at 8 m/s. You run horizontally at 6 m/s. At what angle should you tilt your umbrella from vertical to stay dry?

options.text: 45° from vertical
Straight up — rain falls vertically
arctan(6/8) ≈ 36.9° toward the direction you're running — the rain appears to come at you from an angle
arctan(8/6) ≈ 53.1° from vertical

quiz_questions.explanation: In your (moving) frame, rain has a horizontal component of 6 m/s and vertical component of 8 m/s. Angle from vertical: θ = arctan(6/8) = arctan(0.75) ≈ 36.9°.

quiz_questions.question_text: A child sitting in a train moving at constant velocity drops a ball. According to Galilean relativity, in the train's frame the ball falls straight down. What does an observer on the platform see?

options.text: The ball falls straight down
The ball curves backward relative to the train
The ball follows a parabolic path — it has the train's horizontal velocity plus downward gravitational acceleration
The ball moves only horizontally

quiz_questions.explanation: In the ground frame, the ball has the train's constant horizontal velocity and accelerates vertically due to gravity, producing a parabolic trajectory — just like any horizontal projectile.

quiz_questions.question_text: A helicopter flies at 40 m/s east relative to the ground. A package is dropped from the helicopter. What is the package's initial velocity relative to the ground?

options.text: 40 m/s west
0 m/s — it was dropped, not thrown
40 m/s east — the package inherits the helicopter's horizontal velocity at the moment of release
40 m/s downward

quiz_questions.explanation: When released, the package has the helicopter's velocity (40 m/s east). 'Dropped' means no extra velocity was added, but the package retains its existing motion — Galilean velocity addition applies.

quiz_questions.question_text: Car A moves north at 30 m/s. Car B moves south at 20 m/s. Car C is parked. Rank the speed of Car A as measured by each observer (C's frame, B's frame).

options.text: C measures 30 m/s, B measures 10 m/s
Both measure 30 m/s
C measures 30 m/s, B measures 50 m/s — Car A appears fastest from the approaching Car B's frame
C measures 50 m/s, B measures 30 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: From C (parked): v_A/C = 30 m/s. From B (approaching): v_A/B = 30 − (−20) = 50 m/s. Objects approaching each other have higher relative speeds.

quiz_questions.question_text: An espionage movie shows a car chase where the hero's car goes 50 m/s east and the villain's car goes 45 m/s east. The hero fires a projectile backward at 30 m/s relative to his car. What speed does the villain's driver see the projectile approaching?

options.text: 80 m/s
30 m/s
25 m/s — projectile's ground speed = 50 − 30 = 20 m/s east; relative to villain: 20 − 45 = −25 m/s, so it approaches at 25 m/s
5 m/s

quiz_questions.explanation: Projectile ground velocity = 50 − 30 = 20 m/s east. Villain's velocity = 45 m/s east. v_projectile/villain = 20 − 45 = −25 m/s (25 m/s approaching, since the villain is faster than the projectile's ground speed).
